blocks.text: For if Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about, but not before God.
For what does the scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness.”
3 : Gen 15.6 (also in Gal 3.6; and Jas 2.23, which draws a different conclusion on the basis of this scripture).
Now to one who works, wages are not reckoned as a gift but as something due.
But to one who without works trusts him who justifies the ungodly, such faith is reckoned as righteousness.

5 : refers to God's unique ability to reckon the ungodly righteous (see also v. 6). includes all humanity (3.19–20,23). refers to faith as exemplified in Abraham: faith that in recognition that being made righteous (justified) is the work of God, not the consequence of , that is, human striving to be good in God's sight; translates the same Gk word as “believed” in 4.3.
So also David speaks of the blessedness of those to whom God reckons righteousness apart from works:
“Blessed are those whose iniquities are forgiven, and whose sins are covered;
blessed is the one against whom the Lord will not reckon sin.”

6–8 : Paul supports his interpretation of Genesis by quoting Ps 32.1–2. God's grace is seen in God's willingness to here is transgression of God's will for humanity to do only good. is a commercial term meaning to repay. Paul contrasts that sort of transaction with the gift of justification/being made righteous (3.24).
Is this blessedness, then, pronounced only on the circumcised, or also on the uncircumcised? We say, “Faith was reckoned to Abraham as righteousness.”
9 : See v. 3n.
How then was it reckoned to him? Was it before or after he had been circumcised? It was not after, but before he was circumcised.

He received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised. The purpose was to make him the ancestor of all who believe without being circumcised and who thus have righteousness reckoned to them,

10–11 : Abraham was justified (Gen 15.6) (Gen 17.24). Paul uses the sequence of the Genesis story to bolster his claim that the blessedness of being reckoned righteous on the basis of faith is available to whether or not they are (that is, are Jews).
and likewise the ancestor of the circumcised who are not only circumcised but who also follow the example of the faith that our ancestor Abraham had before he was circumcised.
4.1–12: Abraham made righteous by faith.
12 : The may also have righteousness reckoned to them, as long as they follow Abraham's , that is, his trust in God's capacity to give righteousness to the ungodly (4.5).
For the promise that he would inherit the world did not come to Abraham or to his descendants through the law but through the righteousness of faith.
13 : Paul echoes Jewish interpretation of God's promise to Abraham (Gen 12.3) as being the inheritance of ( . 19.21; cf. . 14.13). , that is, righteousness that comes through faith.
If it is the adherents of the law who are to be the heirs, faith is null and the promise is void.
For the law brings wrath; but where there is no law, neither is there violation.
15 : here refers to God's response to transgression of the law (cf. the curses on those who disobey the law in Lev 27; Deut 28). Paul offers a different perspective on God's wrath at 1.18.

For this reason it depends on faith, in order that the promise may rest on grace and be guaranteed to all his descendants, not only to the adherents of the law but also to those who share the faith of Abraham (for he is the father of all of us,
as it is written, “I have made you the father of many nations”)—in the presence of the God in whom he believed, who gives life to the dead and calls into existence the things that do not exist.

17 : Gen 17.5. , alludes both to Abraham's dead body (4.19) and God's raising Jesus from the dead (4.24). , refers not only to the birth of Isaac but to God's capacity to bring righteousness where it does not exist. Paul may also be working with the conviction that God created all out of nothing; see 2 Macc 7.28; . 21.4; 48.8.
Hoping against hope, he believed that he would become “the father of many nations,” according to what was said, “So numerous shall your descendants be.”
He did not weaken in faith when he considered his own body, which was already as good as dead (for he was about a hundred years old), or when he considered the barrenness of Sarah's womb.
No distrust made him waver concerning the promise of God, but he grew strong in his faith as he gave glory to God,
20 : , lit., “lack of faith.”
being fully convinced that God was able to do what he had promised.
Therefore his faith “was reckoned to him as righteousness.”
22 : See vv. 3n.,9.
Now the words, “it was reckoned to him,” were written not for his sake alone,
but for ours also. It will be reckoned to us who believe in him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead,
who was handed over to death for our trespasses and was raised for our justification.
4.13–25: God's promise to Abraham extends to all who share his faith.

25 : , the same Gk word translated “gave up” in 1.24,26,28; see 1.24n. Elsewhere Paul describes as an enemy (1 Cor 15.26). See also 1 Cor 11.23 and Isa 53.4–5,11–12. , on account of humanity's sins. , for the sake of humanity being made righteous.
